headline: Tom’s clarity and Principal impact are low because he’s staying in safe execution while the team’s Principal bar still feels “fuzzy.”
summary_bullets: Tom can make independent calls, but he “over-index[es] on being safe” and avoids “forcing cross-team decisions,” so Principal impact isn’t visible yet.
He directly links the questioning gap to missing calibration: “I need explicit expectations for the next cycle so I can calibrate fast.”
He recognizes the core mechanism to change next: he will “write down the decision rule” from a deliverable before the next check-in.
understanding_paragraph: Tom understands the problem is not technical—it’s visible ownership and leverage at Principal scope. He’s aware he’s under-questioning and staying in-lane, and he attributes it to needing sharper expectations and earlier feedback. The new signal is that he can name a concrete process change (decision-rule capture), not just general intent.

axes.meaning: No wellbeing signal in this session—no stress, recovery, or sustainability data to act on.
Weak-to-moderate engagement signal: he’s shipping, but not aiming at highest-leverage work and he over-indexes on safety.
Strong clarity gap: the team’s Principal bar feels “fuzzy,” and he needs explicit expectations to calibrate.
Real growth friction: he’s not building cross-team trust fast enough and he delays sharper feedback and visible ownership.

brutal_truth_employee: Tom’s own read is that he’s not hitting the Principal bar: “I am shipping, but I am not sure I am aiming at the highest-leverage work yet.” He admits the behavior that blocks it: “I have mostly stayed in my lane instead of forcing cross-team decisions,” and “I still over-index on being safe.”
brutal_truth_manager: When Tom said, “I can describe Principal impact in general, but this team's bar still feels fuzzy,” the conversation needed a tighter translation into this team’s concrete outcomes and decision points. The key moment to deepen was his admission about calibration—“I need explicit expectations for the next cycle”—so you could lock those expectations to observable artifacts and dates, not just acknowledge the need.

next_actions.action: Email Tom a one-page “Principal bar” for this team: 3 observable outcomes, 2 cross-team decisions expected, and the exact check-in dates for each.
Schedule a 30-minute working session with Tom to pick one recent deliverable and write the decision rule together, then define what “sharper feedback earlier” looks like.
Start the 1:1 by asking Tom to show the decision-rule writeup and name one adjacent-team trust move he made before the next check-in.

watch_for: Before next 1:1: if Tom cannot point to a written decision rule tied to a specific deliverable, treat the calibration plan as not executed.
Within two weeks: if Tom continues “staying in my lane” without at least one forced cross-team decision, the safety bias is persisting.
